summary:
Hepatic steatosis drives insulin resistance via elevated free fatty acid levels causing excessive DNL; chronic inflammatory responses contribute to progressive hepatocellular damage potentially culminating in NAFLD/NASH/cirrhosis cycles initiated primarily due to initial IR-induced processes.
Hepatic steatosis acts centrally in inducing and maintaining insulin resistance in liver disorders mainly through enhanced FFA influx promoting excess DNL while sustained inflammasome activation leads toward advancing pathologies including but not limited to NASH progression up until potential end-stage conditions such as cirrhotic states. The primary driver remains early onset IR setting these sequential events motioning forward clinically observable manifestations across spectrum severity depending upon individual patient factors influencing overall prognosis trajectory dynamics postulated here based extensive current medical knowledge base available today regarding interplay between metabolism & systemic health status particularly focusing on critical roles played by adipose tissue dysfunction alongside other contributing elements discussed above comprehensively addressing core aspects pertinent directly related questions posed at outset concerning mechanisms underlying development/progression patterns observed frequently encountered scenarios characterized broadly speaking under umbrella term 'liver disease'. To condense:

Insulin resistance causes hepatic steatosis by increasing FFAs, triggering excessive DNL; persistent inflammation then progresses pathology likely resulting in NASH/CIRRHOSIS. Key point: Steatohepatitis starts with IR-driven fat buildup expanding progressively unless managed effectively preventing serious complications. Summary: 
- Increased free fatty acids lead to higher de novo lipogenesis, fostering hepatic steatosis.
- Chronic inflammation follows, progressing to possible NASH or cirrhosis. 

Key takeaway: Initial insulin resistance sets off chain reactions ultimately impacting liver function significantly over time. Agent believes option B correctly identifies hepatic steatosis as crucial link explaining how insulin resistance develops in liver diseases. To summarize only the key clinical points about why insulin resistance occurs in liver disease according to the agent’s reasoning:

Increased free fatty acids enter liver → Excessive de novo lipogenesis (FFA) promotes hepatic steatosis  
Chronic inflammation ensues → Progresses to Non-Alcoholic Steato-Hepatitis (NASH)
→ Potentially evolves into Cirrohsis/Failure if left unmanaged
